name: Python Code Quality Rubric
description: Evaluates Python source code for correctness, design quality, error handling, security hygiene, documentation, and maintainability. Designed for code produced by AI agents or humans.

criteria.criterion: No logic errors — off-by-one, wrong comparison operators, or unreachable branches
criteria.evidence_required: Quote the exact line(s) containing the logic error and explain what the code does vs. what it should do
criteria.why: Logic errors cause silent incorrect behavior or runtime failures that are often hard to trace back to their source (PEP 20: 'Errors should never pass silently')

criteria.criterion: Return values that can be None are checked before use at call sites
criteria.evidence_required: Quote the function signature or return statement showing None is possible, then quote the call site that uses the value without a None check
criteria.why: Unguarded None dereferences are among the most common runtime exceptions in Python; Optional propagation must be explicit

criteria.criterion: Exception handling is appropriately scoped — no bare except, overly broad except Exception, or swallowed errors
criteria.evidence_required: Quote the except clause and show that it either hides a specific exception type, silently swallows errors without logging, or uses a bare except that catches BaseException
criteria.why: Overly broad exception handling turns detectable bugs into silent failures and makes diagnosis extremely difficult (PEP 20: 'Errors should never pass silently')

criteria.criterion: Data type consistency — function signatures match actual return types and Optional types propagate correctly
criteria.evidence_required: Quote the function signature (or inferred return type) and then quote a code path that returns a value inconsistent with the declared or expected type
criteria.why: Type inconsistencies cause AttributeError, TypeError, or subtle behavioral bugs that only surface at runtime under specific conditions (PEP 484: Type Hints, PEP 526: Variable Annotations)

criteria.criterion: Single Responsibility — functions and classes do not mix unrelated concerns
criteria.evidence_required: Quote the function or class definition and identify two or more distinct unrelated responsibilities it handles (e.g., parsing + persistence + formatting in one function)
criteria.why: SRP violations create tight coupling, make testing harder, and cause unintended side effects when changing one concern (PEP 20: 'There should be one-- and preferably only one --obvious way to do it')

criteria.criterion: Appropriate abstraction level — no God functions over 50 lines mixing concerns, no unnecessary complexity
criteria.evidence_required: Quote the function header and state its approximate line count, describing the multiple concerns it conflates
criteria.why: Excessively large functions are cognitively unmaintainable and resist refactoring, testing, and safe modification (PEP 20: 'Simple is better than complex', 'Readability counts')

criteria.criterion: No significant code duplication — near-duplicate logic should be extracted into reusable functions
criteria.evidence_required: Quote two or more near-identical code blocks (showing the duplicated logic) that could be unified into a single parameterized function
criteria.why: Code duplication creates divergence risk: one copy gets fixed or updated while others remain buggy (PEP 20: 'There should be one-- and preferably only one --obvious way to do it')

criteria.criterion: Dependencies are injected, not hardcoded — no global singletons or module-level instantiation that prevents testing
criteria.evidence_required: Quote the hardcoded dependency (e.g., direct module import with instantiation, global client object) and explain what makes it impossible to mock or substitute in tests
criteria.why: Hardcoded dependencies couple business logic to infrastructure, making unit tests require live external systems (PEP 20: 'Complex is better than complicated')

criteria.criterion: Resource lifecycle is complete — files, connections, and locks are closed in all code paths including exceptions
criteria.evidence_required: Quote the resource acquisition (open(), connect(), acquire()) and show the code path where close/release is not guaranteed (missing try/finally or context manager)
criteria.why: Resource leaks cause file descriptor exhaustion, connection pool starvation, and deadlocks under load or error conditions

criteria.criterion: Caught exceptions include sufficient diagnostic context before re-raising or logging
criteria.evidence_required: Quote the except block showing an exception caught and re-raised or logged without preserving the original traceback or adding contextual information
criteria.why: Context-free exception handling hides the root cause and makes incident diagnosis require reproduction rather than log inspection

criteria.criterion: Component failures degrade gracefully — one failing component does not unnecessarily cascade to the whole system
criteria.evidence_required: Quote the code path where a single component failure propagates an unhandled exception that terminates a broader process that could have continued
criteria.why: Unnecessary cascading failures turn partial problems into total outages, reducing system resilience

criteria.criterion: User or external input is validated and sanitized before use
criteria.evidence_required: Quote the code that receives external input (function parameter, request body, file read, environment variable) and show it is used without validation, type coercion, or bounds checking
criteria.why: Unvalidated input is the root cause of injection attacks, unexpected crashes, and data corruption

criteria.criterion: Error messages do not disclose internal paths, stack traces, or system details to external consumers
criteria.evidence_required: Quote the error handler or response construction that includes a full traceback, absolute file path, or internal system identifier in a user-facing or API-facing message
criteria.why: Information disclosure aids attackers in mapping internal system structure and identifying exploitable components

criteria.criterion: No unsafe patterns — eval/exec not used on untrusted input, pickle not used on untrusted sources, subprocess not called with shell=True and variable interpolation
criteria.evidence_required: Quote the exact call to eval(), exec(), pickle.loads(), or subprocess with shell=True, and show that the argument includes external or user-controlled data
criteria.why: These patterns are direct code execution vectors — they allow arbitrary code execution if any attacker-controlled input reaches them

criteria.criterion: Docstrings are accurate — they describe what the code actually does, not an earlier or different version
criteria.evidence_required: Quote the docstring and then quote the code that contradicts it, explaining the specific discrepancy
criteria.why: Inaccurate docstrings are worse than no docstrings — they actively mislead maintainers and API consumers (PEP 257: Docstring Conventions)

criteria.criterion: Variable and function names are clear and unambiguous — no misleading names or single-letter variables outside of accepted idioms
criteria.evidence_required: Quote the variable or function name and explain what it actually contains vs. what the name implies, or why the name is too ambiguous to understand without reading the implementation
criteria.why: Misleading names are a form of technical debt that causes incorrect assumptions and subtle bugs during maintenance (PEP 8: Naming Conventions, PEP 20: 'Readability counts')

criteria.criterion: No magic numbers or strings — hardcoded literals with domain significance should be named constants
criteria.evidence_required: Quote the hardcoded literal and explain what it represents semantically and why it should be a named constant (e.g., timeout values, status codes, buffer sizes)
criteria.why: Magic literals are invisible — they make code unreadable and cause errors when the value needs changing and not all occurrences are found (PEP 8: Constants, PEP 20: 'Readability counts')

criteria.criterion: Async/sync boundaries are respected — no blocking calls inside async functions that would block the event loop
criteria.evidence_required: Quote the async function definition and the synchronous blocking call within it (e.g., time.sleep(), requests.get(), open() for large files, subprocess.run()) that would block the event loop
criteria.why: Blocking calls in async functions starve the event loop, causing all concurrent tasks to freeze until the blocking operation completes

criteria.criterion: Shared mutable state accessed from multiple threads or async tasks is protected by appropriate synchronization
criteria.evidence_required: Quote the shared mutable object (dict, list, counter, etc.) and show two or more code paths that read and write it from different threads or tasks without a lock, mutex, or thread-safe structure
criteria.why: Unsynchronized shared state causes race conditions that manifest as data corruption, lost updates, or crashes under concurrent load

criteria.criterion: asyncio.CancelledError is not swallowed by broad except clauses
criteria.evidence_required: Quote the except clause (e.g., except Exception) that would catch CancelledError and show there is no re-raise of CancelledError or check for it
criteria.why: Swallowing CancelledError prevents cooperative cancellation in asyncio, causing tasks to run past their intended lifetime and making graceful shutdown impossible

criteria.criterion: LLM API response fields are validated before access — no direct field access on unverified response structure
criteria.evidence_required: Quote the code that accesses response.choices[0].message.content or similar path without checking that the response, choices list, and nested fields are non-null and of expected shape
criteria.why: LLM APIs can return malformed responses, empty choices lists, or content=None under error conditions; unguarded access causes AttributeError or IndexError

criteria.criterion: Transient API failures are handled with retry and exponential backoff logic
criteria.evidence_required: Quote the API call site and show there is no retry wrapper, tenacity decorator, or manual retry loop around it — or that the error handler reraises immediately without backoff
criteria.why: Transient network and rate-limit errors (429, 503) are normal for LLM/API calls; without retry logic, temporary failures become hard failures

criteria.criterion: User or external input is not interpolated directly into LLM prompts without sanitization
criteria.evidence_required: Quote the prompt construction showing user-controlled or external data interpolated via f-string or concatenation without any filtering, escaping, or structural separation from instructions
criteria.why: Direct interpolation of untrusted input into prompts is a prompt injection vector — attackers can override instructions, extract system prompts, or cause unexpected model behavior

criteria.criterion: LLM calls specify max_tokens or equivalent budget controls to prevent runaway token usage
criteria.evidence_required: Quote the LLM API call and show that max_tokens (or max_completion_tokens, or equivalent provider parameter) is not set, leaving token usage unbounded
criteria.why: Unbounded LLM calls can generate extremely long outputs, causing unexpected cost spikes and timeout failures in production

criteria.criterion: Functions separate pure logic from I/O side effects to enable unit testing without mocks
criteria.evidence_required: Quote the function that mixes computation with I/O (file reads/writes, network calls, database queries, random number generation) in a way that makes it impossible to test the logic without executing or mocking the I/O
criteria.why: Functions that cannot be tested in isolation require integration test infrastructure for even basic logic verification, slowing development and reducing coverage (PEP 20: 'Simple is better than complex')
